role_summary: Schoolteacher at Marshall High School in Belzoni, Mississippi and later in Tchula, Mississippi (Holmes County); mother of one of the children who attempted to integrate the Tchula public school in 1965 (the school was burned down twice in response); later elected to the Tchula school board.
ai_reading: Burns's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 78), with Robert G. Clark Jr. (entry 107, the first African American in the Mississippi House since Reconstruction and also a Holmes County native) as the top match and the broader cluster including Rosie Head (entry 111), Walter Bruce (entry 123, also Holmes County), and Euvester Simpson (entry 41). The Clark and Bruce pairings are the catalog's clearest Holmes County, Mississippi sustained-organizing-tradition cosines: Burns, Bruce, and Clark all worked the same county's voter-registration and school-integration arc, and Clark himself emerged from that organizing tradition into the Mississippi legislature. The novel observation her page surfaces is the teacher-as-organizer-under-job-threat signature: Burns describes how her teaching position at Marshall High School was threatened when she associated with SNCC members, the catalog's clearest documentation of the economic-retaliation pressure on Black professionals during Mississippi civil rights organizing, distinct from the front-line-organizer mode.
biographical_paragraph: While Burns's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 70 [src: 1]. Burns grew up in segregated schools in Humphreys County, Mississippi, and became a schoolteacher at Marshall High School in Belzoni, Mississippi (the Humphreys County seat). She describes how she took notice of the civil rights movement but limited her direct involvement because of the risk to her teaching job; her husband was active in civil rights work and her own job was threatened when she associated with SNCC members [src: 1]. In 1965 her son and other African American children attempted to integrate the white school in Tchula, Mississippi (Holmes County); the school was burned down twice by segregationists, and Burns documents both the protests by white residents against the integration and her own subsequent experience as a teacher in Tchula. She later won election to the Tchula school board and worked from that institutional position [src: 1].
movement_context: Holmes County, Mississippi, the county Burns moved to after Belzoni and where she later won election to the Tchula school board, was one of the first Mississippi Delta counties to develop sustained independent Black voter-registration organizing in the early 1960s, and it did so on a base of Black landowning farmers in the Mileston resettlement community (a New Deal Farm Security Administration land-purchase program of the late 1930s that placed Black tenant farmers on owner-occupied parcels) rather than through the more typical Delta plantation-tenant base [src: 2, 3]. The Holmes County Voters League formed in April 1963 when fourteen Black farmers from the Mileston, Sunny Mount, and Second Pilgrim's Rest communities went to the Lexington courthouse to attempt to register, and the County's Black-led organizing infrastructure expanded from that nucleus through 1963 and 1964 [src: 3]. SNCC field secretaries Hollis Watkins and John Ball worked through the county during this period as part of the broader Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) voter-registration project [src: 3]. The school-integration arc Burns documents from inside the Tchula school district was a direct outgrowth of the County's organizing base: the 1965-1966 freedom-of-choice integration in Holmes County, opened by African American families willing to enroll their children in previously all-white schools under the Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VI compliance framework, was met with white-resistance violence including the school-burning incidents in Tchula Burns describes from her own family experience [src: 1, 3]. The County's organizing base also produced Robert G. Clark Jr., a Holmes County native (born in Ebenezer) and educator who in November 1967 won election to the Mississippi House of Representatives as the first African American to hold a seat in that chamber since Reconstruction, running on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party ticket from the Holmes County-anchored District 16; Clark went on to serve in the Mississippi House continuously through 2004 and rose to become Speaker pro tempore from 1992 onward [src: 4]. Humphreys County, where Burns taught at Marshall High School in Belzoni earlier in her career, was the site of the May 7, 1955 assassination of NAACP voter-registration organizer the Reverend George W. Lee outside Belzoni (one of the Delta cases that mobilized national attention on Mississippi anti-Black political violence in the year leading into the Emmett Till murder in Tallahatchie County that August), and the August 1955 attempted assassination of NAACP organizer Gus Courts in the same town [src: 5]. The teacher-as-organizer-under-job-threat pattern Burns names in her oral history was a documented feature of the economic-retaliation infrastructure white Citizens' Council organizations and county school boards across the Mississippi Delta operated against Black professionals whose households associated with civil rights work; Black schoolteachers were particularly exposed because their salaries were paid from county school-board budgets that white officials controlled, and the dismissal of teachers whose family members signed voter-registration affidavits or attended NAACP or SNCC meetings was the principal sanction available to those officials [src: 2, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Independent scholarly or institutional reception of Julia Matilda Burns specifically is not established in the standard secondary literature on the Mississippi civil rights movement as of this iteration of the catalog. Her Library of Congress oral history is the principal extant first-person record [src: 1]. The Holmes County organizing arc her testimony documents from inside the school-integration experience is treated in the scholarly literature primarily through the Susan Lorenzi and the Lorenzi family's papers held at the Wisconsin Historical Society (Susan Lorenzi worked in Holmes County as a SNCC and COFO staff member through 1964 and 1965 and her papers contain extensive documentation of the County's voter-registration and school-integration work) and through the Charles M. Payne and John Dittmer accounts of the Holmes County base in the broader Mississippi-movement scholarship [src: 2, 6]. The conservative reading of the available record is that her oral history is the documentary anchor; further biographical and institutional reception work would require archival research at the Mississippi Department of Archives and History in Jackson or at the Wisconsin Historical Society's SNCC and Lorenzi collections.

gallery.alt: Mississippi state representative Robert G. Clark Jr., portrait from the 1968-1972 Mississippi Official and Statistical Register
gallery.caption: Robert G. Clark Jr., the Holmes County native and educator who in November 1967 won election to the Mississippi House of Representatives as the first African American to hold a seat in that chamber since Reconstruction. Clark is Burns's top semantic-neighbor match in this catalog's embedding; both worked the Holmes County, Mississippi voter-registration and school-integration arc that produced Clark's legislative victory, and Burns's later election to the Tchula school board emerged from the same County organizing infrastructure.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Official and Statistical Register 1968-1972, published by the Mississippi Secretary of State, Jackson, 1972)
gallery.license: Public domain (United States; published between 1931 and 1977 without a copyright notice)

gallery.caption: The Holmes County Courthouse in Lexington, Mississippi, the county-seat building where the Holmes County Voters League's 1963 attempt to register fourteen Black farmers opened the County's sustained voter-registration organizing arc. The Courthouse is on the National Register of Historic Places (reference 94001301); the Confederate Monument visible in the foreground of the postcard view stood on the Courthouse grounds through the period the County's Black organizing infrastructure built up. Burns's later election to the Tchula school board placed her in the institutional framework anchored on this building.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (Cooper Postcard Collection)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History)
gallery.license: Public domain (Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Forrest Lamar Cooper Postcard Collection released into the public domain)

gallery.alt: African Americans fishing in a creek near cotton plantations outside Belzoni, Mississippi, October 1939
gallery.caption: African Americans fishing in a creek near cotton plantations outside Belzoni, Mississippi, photographed by Marion Post Wolcott for the Farm Security Administration in October 1939. Belzoni, the Humphreys County seat where Burns later taught at Marshall High School, was a Delta agricultural town anchored on the cotton economy through the mid-twentieth century; the May 7, 1955 assassination of NAACP voter-registration organizer the Reverend George W. Lee outside the town and the August 1955 attempted assassination of NAACP organizer Gus Courts in the same county made Belzoni one of the earliest documented sites of Mississippi anti-Black political violence in the year leading into the Emmett Till murder.
gallery.photographer: Marion Post Wolcott (1910-1990), Farm Security Administration
gallery.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (via Wikimedia Commons)
gallery.license: Public domain (work of an employee of the United States Farm Security Administration taken as part of official duties; work of the U.S. federal government)

sources.title: Julia Matilda Burns oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994 (Bancroft Prize 1995); WorldCat OCLC 30816494 (the standard institutional history of the Mississippi civil rights movement, including treatment of the Holmes County voter-registration base built on the Mileston resettlement landowning community and the County's organizing infrastructure through the 1960s)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the principal scholarly account of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project and the County-by-County organizing infrastructure, including Holmes County)

sources.title: Robert G. Clark, Robert G. Clark's Journey to the House: A Black Politician's Story
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 52840763 (Clark's first-person account of his 1967 election to the Mississippi House of Representatives from the Holmes County-anchored District 16, the first African American to hold a seat in the chamber since Reconstruction, and his subsequent service through 2003)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer, including treatment of the Belzoni and Humphreys County NAACP organizing the Reverend George W. Lee and Gus Courts led in 1954-1955 in the year leading into the broader Delta voter-registration arc)

sources.title: John Dittmer and Susan Lorenzi, eds., Holmes County, Mississippi: A Local Civil Rights Movement (Wisconsin Historical Society Susan Lorenzi Papers, Mss 595)
sources.publisher: Wisconsin Historical Society Library and Archives, Madison; the Lorenzi family worked in Holmes County as SNCC and COFO staff through 1964 and 1965 and their papers contain extensive documentation of the County's voter-registration and school-integration work, including the freedom-of-choice integration in Tchula and the surrounding County schools that Burns's family experienced from inside

sources.title: Charles W. Eagles, The Price of Defiance: James Meredith and the Integration of Ole Miss
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 53806925 (the principal scholarly history of the white-resistance infrastructure to Mississippi school integration, including the Citizens' Council economic-retaliation apparatus that operated against Black professionals such as schoolteachers whose households associated with civil rights work)

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project, American Folklife Center, About This Collection
